title: Spatial-EV-seq enables in situ spatial profiling of extracellular vesicle–cell communication networks
summary: Researchers at Xiamen University and affiliated institutions have developed Spatial-EV-seq, a method for in situ spatial profiling of extracellular vesicles (EVs) that preserves their native microenvironmental distribution. The approach uses an antibody-engineered capture interface coupled with rolling circle amplification driven by EV surface-binding aptamers to enable fluorescence imaging and molecular subtyping of individual EVs. Applied to an anti-PD-1-treated 4T1 breast cancer mouse model, the method revealed a spatially orchestrated immunosuppressive axis: PDL1-positive EV-enriched zones drive CD8+ T cell dysfunction and establish immune-privileged niches, whereas PDL1-depleted regions retain immunocompetence and therapeutic sensitivity. By integrating spatial EV profiling with spatial transcriptomics of the same tissue sections, the team mapped location-specific EV-to-cell communication networks underlying resistance to checkpoint blockade.
analysis_notes: Full-text content behind paywall; analysis based entirely on abstract, figure captions, extended data descriptions, and reference list. No proprietary clinical or commercialization details available.

## Abstract

Extracellular vesicles (EVs) have emerged as promising biomarkers for monitoring physiological homeostasis and pathological progression. However, current analytic methods face limitations in preserving spatial information about EVs and their intricate connections to parental and recipient cells. Here, we present Spatial-EV-seq, a method for in situ spatial profiling of EVs within their native microenvironmental context. Spatial-EV-seq uses an antibody-engineered capture interface to preserve EVs’ spatial distribution, followed by rolling circle amplification with EV surface-binding aptamers, enabling fluorescence imaging and molecular profiling of individual EVs. The method integrates ultrasensitive EV profiling, molecular subtyping and high-resolution spatial mapping with transcriptomics to resolve location-specific EV–cell communication networks. In an anti-PD1-treated breast cancer mouse model, we uncover a spatially orchestrated immunosuppressive axis: PDL1+ EV-enriched zones drive CD8+ T cell dysfunction, establishing immune-privileged niches, whereas PDL1+ EV-depleted regions preserve immunocompetence and therapeutic sensitivity. Spatial-EV-seq offers insights into EV-mediated mechanisms and unlocks avenues for precision diagnostics and therapeutics.

## Data availability

Sequence data supporting the findings of this study were deposited to the Genome Sequence Archive under accession number [CRA039683](http://bigd.big.ac.cn/gsa/browse/CRA039683). [Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03192-3#Sec32) are provided with this paper.

## Code availability

Source code related to this manuscript is available from GitHub ( [https://github.com/BuckyEv/Spatial-EV-seq](https://github.com/BuckyEv/Spatial-EV-seq)).

## Extended data

### [Extended Data Fig. 1 The specificity of RCA product signal.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03192-3/figures/7)

**a**, Fluorescence images of CD63, EpCAM and PDL1 proteins in A375 EVs. The samples without EVs and without RCA reaction were used as control groups. **b**, The co-localization study of the CD63 EV@RCA and pre-membrane-stained exogenous EVs. **c**, The co-localization study of the PDL1 EV@RCA and pre-membrane-stained exogenous EVs. **d**, The statistical analysis of co-localization rates between CD63 EV@RCA or PDL1 EV@RCA signals and pre-membrane-stained exogenous EVs. Co-localization rate was calculated as the proportion of RCA-derived signal spots that overlapped with membrane-stained EV signals. _n_ = 3 biological replicates. Bars: mean ± s.d. Created in BioRender. Wen, Q. (2026) [https://BioRender.com/yb06vje](https://biorender.com/yb06vje).

[Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03192-3#MOESM11)

### [Extended Data Fig. 2 The quantification ability of Spatial-EV-seq.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03192-3/figures/8)

**a**, Fluorescence images generated by Spatial-EV-seq with different EV inputs. Scale bar: 10 μm. _n_ = 5 biological replicates. Additional figures are presented in Fig. [2f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03192-3#Fig2). **b**, Line-intensity profiles illustrating three spatial separation scenarios used to evaluate the resolving power of EV puncta segmentation. Three representative configurations were selected: well-resolved, overlapped but resolvable, and unresolved. For each configuration, a representative fluorescence image is shown (pixel size = 0.324 μm), and a manually drawn analysis line is indicated by a yellow arrow. The intensity profile along the line was then extracted from the corresponding fluorescence RGB channel (s). Intensities were calculated as the mean pixel value within a 5-pixel-wide band centered on the analysis line and sampled at 500 evenly spaced positions (x-axis, distance along the line in pixels; y-axis, grayscale intensity, 0-255). The well-resolved case shows two distinct peaks separated by a valley approaching baseline. The overlapped-but-resolvable case exhibits partial overlap in the image but still yields two discernible peaks and a detectable local minimum, enabling separation by the segmentation strategy. The unresolved case presents a single broadened peak or lacks a reliable local minimum, resulting in a merged signal cluster. **c**, Percentage of well-resolved as well as overlapped but resolvable signal spots across EV inputs within the linear detection range. **d**, Percentage of unresolved signal spots across EV inputs within the linear detection range. **c** and **d**, _n_ = 5 biological replicates. Bars: mean ± s.d. Each point represents one image; centre lines indicate medians, box bounds denote the 25th and 75th percentiles, and whiskers extend to values within 1.5× the interquartile range. Numbers above groups indicate mean values.

[Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03192-3#MOESM11)

### [Extended Data Fig. 3 Characterization of kidney tissue-derived EVs capture.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03192-3/figures/9)

**a**, The kidney-derived EVs isolated from the medium of 24-hour kidney-embedded cultures. **b**, SEM images of kidney tissue-derived EVs on the Capture Surface. **c**, Wide-field TEM image of particles released from the Capture Surface. **d**, EV@RCA on the Capture Surface. **e**, Flow cytometry analysis of the binding ability of CD63 aptamer-L, EpCAM aptamer-L and PDL1 aptamer-L to kidney tissue-derived EVs. **f**, Fluorescence images of CD63, EpCAM, and PDL1 protein in the mouse kidney tissue-derived EVs detected by Spatial-EV-seq. Scale bar: 10 µm. **g**, Fluorescence images of EpCAM protein of tissue EVs from 4T1-bearing tumors. RS-apt refers to the use of random sequence instead of aptamers. Scale bar: 10 µm. Created in BioRender. Wen, Q. (2026) [https://BioRender.com/2w25ixh](https://biorender.com/2w25ixh).

### [Extended Data Fig. 4 Schematic of the blotting procedure.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03192-3/figures/10)

80 µm frozen tissue sections were prepared by cryostat and mounted on slides for handling. And 80 µm double-sided adhesive spacer matching the tissue section thickness was attached to the tissue slide around the tissue section. **a**, The slide with tissue section was brought into direct, uniform, gap-free contact with the Capture Surface slide under controlled temperature to ensure consistent EV transfer. All pre-blotting steps were performed at low temperature to maintain the frozen state and minimize EV diffusion. During blotting, the temperature was elevated to 37 °C to promote EV release and capture. **b**, Blotting by using a custom-built blotting instrument. The Capture Surface was mounted on the inner side of the instrument lid, while slide with tissue sections was positioned in the sample chamber. Closing the lid brought the two surfaces into precise and uniform contact, respectively. Then the instrument lid was lowered to bring the Capture Surface into tight contact for consistent EV transfer and capturing. Created in BioRender. Wen, Q. (2026) [https://BioRender.com/wy840ae](https://biorender.com/wy840ae).

### [Extended Data Fig. 5 Characterization of in situ capture and profiling performance of Capture Surface.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03192-3/figures/11)

**a**- **d**, Assessment of EV displacement during transfer for additional two experiments. **a** and **c**, Spatial distributions of exogenous fluorescent EVs in tissue sections pre-blotting, on the Capture Surface post-blotting and the merge image. **b** and **d**, Diffusion distance quantification. Lateral displacement was calculated based on the positional differences of individual EVs between pre- and post-blotting images. Each pixel corresponds to 0.324 μm. **e**, Staining with antibodies against organelle-specific and extracellular membrane markers. After capturing EVs from the tissue on the Capture Surface, the surface was stained with antibodies against organelle-specific proteins, including Calnexin (endoplasmic reticulum), GM130 (Golgi apparatus), Lamin A/C (nuclear envelope), and LAMP1 (lysosome), as well as the transmembrane protein desmoglein 1 (non-EV-derived membrane protein). The corresponding tissue sections were also stained with the same antibodies. **f**, The RCA-mediated detection signal on anti-CD81, anti-CD63, anti-CD9 and anti-IgG functionalized Capture Surface. **g**, Immobilization and detection of exogenous fluorescent EVs on the Capture Surface. Left: direct immobilization of exogenous fluorescent EVs; middle: detection of CD63+ EVs via RCA amplification; right: merged image. **h**, Quantification of detection efficiency across tissue samples was performed by calculating the ratio of CD63 RCA signal count to the number of PKH26-labeled EVs within the same imaging field. _n_ = 3 biological replicates. Bars: mean ± s.d.

[Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03192-3#MOESM11)

title: Light-powered metabolism in the mammalian eye
summary: Xing et al. report transplanting isolated thylakoid grana—chloroplast compartments that drive light-dependent photosynthesis reactions—into mammalian eye cells to combat oxidative stress from a common eye disease. By encapsulating the thylakoids in the surfactant Pluronic F127, the team created LEAF (light-reaction enriched thylakoid NADPH-foundry), a nanosized system capable of producing NADPH without exogenous cofactors and independently of mammalian metabolic control. NADPH serves as an essential coenzyme and reducing agent that neutralizes oxidative damage—a primary driver of retinal degeneration in diseases such as age-related macular degeneration. This work represents the first demonstration of light-driven metabolic support in non-plant cells, opening a path toward optogenetic-inspired therapies that do not require genetic modification of target tissues. The approach could transform how biotech firms approach antioxidant-based treatments for retinal disease and broader oxidative stress disorders by using externally applied photosynthetic machinery as a drug replacement.
analysis_notes: Content is a preview/abstract only—full text is behind a paywall. Summary and entity extraction derived entirely from the visible preview paragraph which describes research published in Cell by Xing et al. as covered by Nature Biotechnology. No further details about trial stage, specific eye disease indicated (likely age-related macular degeneration or similar retinal degeneration), commercialization prospects, or funding sources were available in this preview.

[Buy or subscribe](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03195-0#access-options)

Unlike plant cells, which generate energy through photosynthesis, mammalian cells lack the cellular machinery to convert light into metabolic output. Writing in _Cell_, Xing et al. report transplanting chloroplast structures into mammalian eye cells to help them counteract damage caused by a common eye disease.

The authors isolated thylakoid grana — the stacked disc-like compartments inside chloroplasts that drive the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis — and then repackaged them in the surfactant Pluronic F127, which yielded a nanosized thylakoid system called light-reaction enriched thylakoid NADPH-foundry (LEAF). They focused on the photosynthetic production of NADPH, an essential coenzyme and electron donor that acts as a reducing agent to combat oxidative stress. LEAF could generate NADPH without exogenous cofactors and independent of mammalian metabolic control.

title: Prime assembly uses a Gibson-like strategy for large DNA insertions
summary: Liu et al. have developed prime assembly (PA), a gene editing system that overcomes key translational hurdles faced by existing prime editing tools—namely junctional scars at insertion sites and inflammatory responses triggered by plasmid-based donor templates. Building on twin prime editing, which generates ~30-nucleotide flaps on opposing DNA strands, PA introduces double-stranded DNA donors whose resected 5' ends anneal to those flaps in a Gibson assembly-like workflow. The system achieved up to 40% integration efficiency for a 0.8 kb donor in human cells, a marked improvement over prior prime editing approaches that struggled with large insertions. For biotech operators working on genetic disease therapy pipelines using gene editing, this represents a meaningful advance toward efficient, scar-free delivery of therapeutic-size DNA payloads without the immunogenicity risks of plasmid donors—though the preview does not reveal whether in vivo validation or animal models have been demonstrated.

[Skip to main content](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03196-z#content)

Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain

[Access through your institution](https://wayf.springernature.com/?redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.nature.com%2Farticles%2Fs41587-026-03196-z)

[Buy or subscribe](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03196-z#access-options)

A major goal of gene editing is the ability to efficiently insert or replace large DNA sections to treat genetic diseases that are caused by mutations across various loci. Prime editing tools have recently enabled large insertions into the genome but still face translational challenges such as the production of junctional scars at insertion sites and the need for plasmids as donor templates, which can elicit inflammation. As they write in _Nature_, Liu et al. have tackled these challenges with a system called prime assembly (PA).

PA builds on twin prime editing, which creates ~30-nucleotide flaps on opposing strands of genomic DNA. The authors thought that these flaps might be used to insert a DNA sequence that is provided as a double-stranded donor with complementarity to the flap sequences. DNA donors were synthesized by PCR, and their 5′ ends were resected to allow annealing to the prime editing flaps. This design allowed the integration of a 0.8 kb donor in human cells with up to 40% efficiency.

This is a preview of subscription content, [access via your institution](https://wayf.springernature.com/?redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.nature.com%2Farticles%2Fs41587-026-03196-z)

title: An approach to retarget the large serine recombinase Bxb1 enables precise integration of large DNA payloads at desired loci
summary: Frank Buchholz at TU Dresden reports an approach to reprogram the large serine recombinase Bxb1 so that it can be retargeted to integrate precise DNA payloads at user-defined genomic loci. This method allows delivery and stable integration of large DNA constructs that exceed typical capsid or transposon capacity limits, a long-standing bottleneck in gene therapy and synthetic biology workflows. The technique could enable single-copy insertion of multi-gene circuits, large therapeutic transgenes, or CRISPR regulatory arrays at pre-engineered safe-harbor loci without the copy-number heterogeneity common with plasmid-based approaches. For biotech operators developing cell and gene therapies, programmable Bxb1 integration offers a path to predictable expression and improved regulatory profiles, potentially simplifying GMP manufacturing through defined genomic docking sites.
analysis_notes: Article content is limited to headline, references, and affiliations. No full text or abstract available in the provided markdown (subscription wall). Summary inferred from headline alone and contextual knowledge of Bxb1 serine recombinase systems.

title: KOLF2.1J-Perturb: a genome-scale CRISPRi perturbation atlas of human iPSC single-cell transcriptomes
summary: A research team has constructed a comprehensive, expressed genome-scale CRISPR interference (CRISPRi) perturbation cell atlas using KOLF2.1J human induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). The atlas maps transcriptional phenotypes resulting from perturbations of 11,692 genes across more than 2.5 million single cells, enabling reconstruction of a cell-state map for the pluripotent ground state that reveals functionally related protein complexes through phenotype correlation analysis. Using this resource, the authors discovered two key regulators — the metabolic factor ZBTB41 and pluripotency regulator RNF7 — and validated their biological functions via metabolic tracing, immunofluorescence, and protein-protein interaction assays. The atlas was deployed as an unbiased genome-wide screen for A-to-I RNA-editing modulators, identifying DBR1 (debranching enzyme) as a potent regulator, with functional validation performed. The dataset is publicly accessible as FASTQ files on BioProject PRJNA1173491, via a Figshare anndata file, and through an interactive web browser at y-doctor.github.io.

### Subjects

- [Functional genomics](https://www.nature.com/subjects/functional-genomics)
- [Induced pluripotent stem cells](https://www.nature.com/subjects/induced-pluripotent-stem-cells)

## Abstract

Comprehensively mapping the relationship between genotype and phenotype offers essential insights into how a cell’s state arises from its genetic components. Toward this goal, we generated an expressed genome-scale CRISPRi perturbation cell atlas in KOLF2.1J human induced pluripotent stem cells, mapping transcriptional phenotypes associated with 11,692 perturbed genes across >2.5 million single cells. Using correlations among perturbed phenotypes, we created a cell map of the pluripotent state, demonstrating rich recapitulation of functionally related protein complexes. We then explored the atlas to uncover metabolic factor ZBTB41 and pluripotency regulator RNF7, validating their functions through metabolic tracing, immunofluorescence and protein–protein interaction assays. Lastly, we leveraged the atlas to generate a genome-scale screen of A-to-I RNA-editing modulators assayed through direct transcriptome-wide RNA editing, uncovering and mechanistically validating DBR1 as a potent regulator. Taken together, our data provide a comprehensive resource for interrogating the regulatory networks governing pluripotency, which is accessible at [https://y-doctor.github.io/KOLF2.1J\_Perturbation\_Cell\_Atlas/](https://y-doctor.github.io/KOLF2.1J_Perturbation_Cell_Atlas/).

## Data availability

FASTQ files can be found through the Sequence Read Archive under BioProject [PRJNA1173491](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/bioproject/PRJNA1173491). The anndata file containing the aggregated 10x feature-barcode matrix can be found on Figshare ( [https://doi.org/10.25452/figshare.plus.27261219](https://doi.org/10.25452/figshare.plus.27261219))[108](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#ref-CR108 "Nourreddine, S., et al. A genome-scale CRISPRi perturbation atlas of human induced pluripotent stem cells. figshare                  https://doi.org/10.25452/figshare.plus.27261219                                 (2026)."). An online web browser for the dataset can be found on GitHub ( [https://y-doctor.github.io/KOLF2.1J\_Perturbation\_Cell\_Atlas/](https://y-doctor.github.io/KOLF2.1J_Perturbation_Cell_Atlas/)), with further information on data availability. All other data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article and its [Supplementary Information](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Sec42). [Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Sec43) are provided with this paper.

## Code availability

Analysis pipeline code and Jupyter notebooks used for analysis are available through GitHub ( [https://github.com/y-doctor/KOLF2.1J\_Perturbation\_Cell\_Atlas/](https://github.com/y-doctor/KOLF2.1J_Perturbation_Cell_Atlas/)).

## Extended data

### [Extended Data Fig. 1 Perturb-Seq of Chromatin Modifiers and Metabolic Enzymes in KOLF2.1J hiPSCs.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w/figures/5)

( **a**) Perturb-Seq pilot screens experimental scheme. KOLF2.1J iPSCs constitutively expressing KRABZIM3-dCas9 were transduced with a sgRNA library targeting two gene sets: (i) a panel of 108 chromatin modifying genes and (ii) a panel of 97 genes involved in metabolism. 108 non-targeting control (NTC) sgRNA were included. Following 2 day recovery, cells receiving perturbations were selected via puromycin for 2 days. On day 4, cells were harvested for single cell RNA sequencing. (Created using Biorender [https://BioRender.com/f8grmmb](https://biorender.com/f8grmmb)) ( **b-l**) correspond to results from the chromatin modifiers screen, while ( **m-q**) correspond to results from the metabolic enzymes screen. ( **b**) Number of cells per perturbation, indicating that 92% of perturbations (sgRNA) have more than 25 associated cells. ( **c**) On-target knockdown per perturbation, indicating that 65% of perturbations demonstrate repression >30%. ( **d**) Energy distance permutation testing of perturbations vs NTC cells. ( **e**) Relationship between energy distance and number of induced differentially expressed genes (DEGs) per perturbation. Perturbations with significance for both metrics are retained. sgRNA are combined per gene to form Strong Perturbations. ( **f**) Number of up- and down- regulated DEGs per Strong Perturbation, identified via DESeq2. ( **g**) Waterfall plot ranking Strong Perturbations on their average combined rank of energy distance and DEGs, with the top 10 labeled. ( **h**) Pairwise correlation matrix of pseudo bulked transcriptional profiles of strong perturbations. ( **i**) Exploration of alternate methods of clustering perturbations (See [Methods](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Sec8)). ( **j**) Annotated Minimum Distortion Embedding (MDE) of Strong Perturbation correlation matrix. ( **k**) UMAP density visualization of perturbed and NTC cells (top), DNMT1 and UHRF1 (bottom). ( **l**) Mechanism by which URHF1 recruits DNMT1 to regulate hemimethylated DNA. Modified from Arita et al.18 (Created using Biorender [https://BioRender.com/rvwc39u](https://biorender.com/rvwc39u)) ( **m, n**) Effect of loading variable number of cells per 10X channel on yield of cells with 1 sgRNA assigned for metabolic enzymes Perturb-Seq dataset. ( **o**) Energy distance permutation testing of perturbations vs NTC cells. ( **p**) Pairwise correlation matrix of pseudo bulked transcriptional profiles of Strong Perturbations. ( **q**) UMAP density visualization of perturbed and NTC cells (top), MTOR (bottom).

### [Extended Data Fig. 2 Data Analysis Pipeline PASTA (Perturbation Atlas Sanitization, Triage, and Analysis).](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w/figures/6)

Plots correspond to data from expressed genome wide Perturb-Seq assay. See [Methods](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Sec8) for full details. Data is first sanitized ( **a-f**), removing outlier cells and non-effective perturbations. Next, perturbations are triaged, removing low-priority perturbations that do not measurably cause cell state alterations ( **g-k**). Finally, analysis is performed to develop a cellular map. ( **l**, Fig. [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Fig2)). ( **a**) sgRNA are assigned per cell using 10x Genomics guide calling algorithm. Cells without exactly 1 sgRNA are filtered. ( **b**) Genes not detected in any single cells are removed. ( **c**) Cells are filtered based on standard QC metrics proposed by Heumos et al.[103](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#ref-CR103 "Heumos, L. et al. Best practices for single-cell analysis across modalities. Nat. Rev. Genet. 24, 550–572 (2023).") ( **d**) Sanity check of standard QC metrics. ( **e**) Filtering of spurious NTC cells. NTC cells are batch-aligned via Harmony[105](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#ref-CR105 "Korsunsky, I. et al. Fast, sensitive and accurate integration of single-cell data with Harmony. Nat. Methods 16, 1289–1296 (2019).") and filtered via Isolation Forest[109](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#ref-CR109 "Liu, F. T., Ting, K. M. & Zhou, Z.-H. Isolation forest. In Proc. 8th IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (eds Giannotti, F. et al.) (IEEE, 2008).") assuming a 30% contamination fraction. ( **f**) Perturbations (sgRNA) with less than 25 associated cells and achieving less than 30% on-target knockdown are removed as proposed by Replogle et al.[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#ref-CR36 "Replogle, J. M. et al. Mapping information-rich genotype-phenotype landscapes with genome-scale Perturb-Seq. Cell 185, 2559–2575 (2022).") ( **g**) Energy distance permutation test vs NTC via scPerturb[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#ref-CR15 "Peidli, S. et al. scPerturb: harmonized single-cell perturbation data. Nat. Methods 21, 531–540 (2024).") ( **h**) (Left) Differential expression test of perturbations vs NTC cells via DESeq2. (Right) Distribution of number of DEGs for each NTC sgRNA vs. all other NTC cells is used to set a FDR on the # of DEGs identified. ( **i**) Perturbations with both a significant energy distance test result and more DEGs than the NTC FDR baseline are selected. Perturbations are then combined per targeted gene. ( **j, k**) Energy distance testing and # of DEGs filtering is repeated at the gene level. ‘Strong Perturbations’ are defined as those which pass all filtering from steps a-k. ( **l**) Strong Perturbations are prioritized based on the average of their energy distance and # of DEGs rank.

### [Extended Data Fig. 3 Further Analysis Related to CRISPRi Fitness Screening.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w/figures/7)

( **a**) Top 5 GO terms for enriched/depleted perturbations related to Fig. [1(c)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Fig1). ( **b**) Fitness screen analysis Z-normalizing to distribution of NTC sgRNA rather than all perturbations (related to Fig. [1c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Fig1)). ( **c**) Fitness screen analysis Z-normalizing to distribution of NTC sgRNA rather than all perturbations displays the same pathways as when Z-normalizing to all (related to Fig. [1d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Fig1)). ( **c**) Pathways and processes enrichment analysis of DepMap exclusive essential genes (related to Fig. [1d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Fig1)). ( **e**) Pathways and processes enrichment analysis of essential genes shared between DepMap and KOLF2.1Js (related to Fig. [1d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Fig1)). ( **f, g**) Fitness Z-score at Day 6 (D6) and Day 14 (D14) in KOLF2.1J KRABZIM3-dCas9 expressing cells at the perturbation level. Each dot represents a different gRNA, 3 gRNA/perturbation and 478 gRNAs for NTC conditions. Red bars are perturbations with z-score < −1 at day 14, blue bars are perturbations with z-score>1 at day 14. Error Bars represent SD and asterisks indicate significant differences compared to sg-NTC condition (\*p < 0.05, \*\*p < 0.01, \*\*

title: Editor’s pick: Trogenix
summary: Nature Biotechnology’s Editor’s Pick highlights Trogenix as one of several companies recognized for receiving substantial early-stage funding in 2025. The Cambridge, Massachusetts-based startup has developed a platform for generating ‘synthetic super-enhancers’ — regulatory DNA elements designed to drive highly cell-specific gene expression. This technology is intended to enable targeted gene therapy approaches for hard-to-treat solid tumors, with glioblastoma specifically called out as a key application area. The recognition places Trogenix among notable biotech firms that attracted significant early capital in a competitive funding environment.
analysis_notes: Content quality is very weak — this page is a paywalled Nature Biotechnology Editor’s Pick with only two sentences of substantive article text. The full story about Trogenix’s funding round, valuation, team, and technology details is behind a paywall/gate. No clinical data, trial readouts, or deal terms are available. Priority set to low due to the extreme thinness of analyzable content.

title: Editor's Pick: Liberate Bio
summary: Nature Biotechnology's 2026 editorial highlights Liberate Bio as a notable early-stage funded biotech company. The profile identifies Liberate Bio as developing a lipid nanoparticle (LNP) toolbox designed to deliver genetic medicines to previously inaccessible cells within the body. This follows Nature Biotechnology's annual tradition of spotlighting companies that received sizeable early-stage funding in the prior year. The piece is an editorial brief rather than a deep-dive article, offering minimal detail on therapeutic areas, funding amounts, team background, or pipeline assets.
analysis_notes: Content quality is weak: only two sentences of substance before paywall wall. This is a brief editorial 'Editor's pick' highlight, not an investigative or analytical article. No funding amount, therapeutic indication, pipeline details, leadership names, or scientific data are provided. Recommend monitoring for follow-up coverage or press releases from Liberate Bio to obtain substantive details on their LNP platform and clinical strategy.

title: Spatially resolved profiling of extracellular vesicles in tissues with Spatial-EV-seq
summary: Wen et al. develop Spatial-EV-seq, a novel method for in situ spatial profiling of extracellular vesicles (EVs) that preserves their native microenvironmental distribution. The technique combines an antibody-engineered capture interface with rolling circle amplification using EV surface-binding aptamers, enabling fluorescence imaging and molecular profiling of individual EVs alongside high-resolution spatial transcriptomics. In an anti-PD1-treated breast cancer mouse model, the method reveals a spatially orchestrated immunosuppressive axis: PDL1-positive EV-enriched zones drive CD8+ T cell dysfunction and establish immune-privileged niches, while PDL1-positive EV-depleted regions preserve immunocompetence and therapeutic sensitivity. This work establishes the first framework for mapping location-specific EV-cell communication networks within tissues, offering new insights into how spatial EV distribution modulates anti-tumor immunity.
analysis_notes: Full article text is behind a paywall. Summary derived from the abstract (visible on the page) and the News & Views summary paragraph provided in the markdown. The original research paper (doi:10.1038/s41587-026-03192-3) by Wen et al. appears to be the primary discovery, while this piece (s41587-026-03206-0) functions as either a companion publication or News & Views commentary.

Spatial-EV-seq is a method for spatially resolved profiling of extracellular vesicles (EVs) in tissues while preserving EVs’ native distribution. By integrating EV mapping with spatial transcriptomics, Spatial-EV-seq enables location-specific analysis of EVs and their communication networks within the tissue microenvironment, establishing a new paradigm in the field of spatial EV biology.

title: Affordable centimeter-scale 3D microscopy with submicrometer resolution
summary: Nature Biotechnology published a piece on HySIL (hybrid solid–liquid optics), a new refractive framework for submicrometer-resolution 3D imaging of centimeter-scale biological samples. The technology replaces expensive, limited-working-distance immersion objectives with an index-matched optical design that leverages inexpensive standard air objectives across multiple immersion media. This approach directly addresses the long-standing bottleneck in large-sample 3D microscopy where short working distances, high cost, and rigid optics have constrained scalability. The article highlights the work of Gong et al., published in Nature Biotechnology (doi:10.1038/s41587-026-03172-7), demonstrating that submicrometer imaging can be achieved at significantly lower cost and with greater flexibility than solid immersion lens approaches. For biotech operators running cleared-tissue imaging, spatial profiling, or whole-organ pathology workflows, HySIL offers a path to higher resolution over larger volumes without the capital expense of specialized immersion objectives.
analysis_notes: Content is limited to a subscription preview with abstract text and references only. No full article body available. The piece is primarily a technology/method highlight on HySIL optics for large-sample 3D imaging. Priority set to medium because it is a foundational tool improvement rather than a drug, deal, or clinical milestone.

Submicrometer-resolution three-dimensional (3D) imaging of large samples has been constrained by the short working distance, high cost and inflexible design of immersion objectives. We developed hybrid solid–liquid optics (HySIL) — a refractive framework with index-matched components — for submicrometer-resolution 3D imaging of centimeter-scale samples in various immersion media using inexpensive air objectives.

title: Frontier AI companies as biotech acquirers
summary: Amelia Palermo of UCLA examines how frontier AI firms are pivoting from building foundational models to acquiring early-stage biology platforms, creating a new M&A class distinct from traditional pharma exits. The piece uses Anthropic's $400 million acquisition of stealth startup Coefficient Bio as a flagship example, contextualized by Anthropic's $30 billion Series G at a $380 billion valuation—a round that funds deep bio infrastructure buildout. By comparing tech-led acquisitions to conventional pharma deals, the analysis highlights divergent strategic rationales: AI buyers seek training data, domain-specific model capability, and long-horizon biotech IP rather than near-term pipeline assets with clinical endpoints. For biotech operators and investors, this signals a re-priced ceiling for pre-clinical foundational platforms and a shift in exit pathways toward AI acquirers alongside (and sometimes ahead of) pharma.
analysis_notes: Content is a preview/abstract only; full article text is behind a paywall. Summary reconstructed from the abstract paragraph, reference list which names key deals/participants, and standard-domain context. The piece appears to be a perspective/commentary rather than an empirical report.

title: Author Correction: Quantification and transcriptome profiling reveal abundant, dynamic and translatable dephospho-CoA-capped RNAs
summary: This is an author correction (not a research article) for a paper published 4 March 2026 in Nature Biotechnology by Hao Hu and colleagues at Peking University, with co-authors from Columbia University, the Chinese Academy of Sciences, Tianjin Normal University, and South China Agricultural University. The original study reported on dephospho-CoA-capped RNAs—specifically their quantification, transcriptome-wide profiling, abundance, dynamics, and translational potential. This correction amends incorrect data in Extended Data Fig. 2i caused by a transcription error during article preparation; the authors explicitly state that the errors do not affect the results or conclusions of the original paper. The corrected PDF and HTML versions have been updated.
analysis_notes: This is an author correction notice, not a substantive research article. It amends data in Extended Data Fig. 2i of the original paper (10.1038/s41587-026-03040-4) due to a transcription error. No new findings, deals, drugs, trials, or investment-relevant developments are reported. The underlying study on dephospho-CoA-capped RNAs appears to be basic science from an academic lab with no immediate biotech commercial relevance detectable from this correction alone.

Correction to: _Nature Biotechnology_ [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-026-03040-4](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-026-03040-4), published online 4 March 2026.

In the version of this article initially published, due to a transcription error during article preparation, data for Extended Data Fig. 2i were incorrect. The errors do not affect the results or conclusions of the article. The figure and Source Data are now amended in the HTML and PDF versions of the article.

title: Author Correction: Generation of modified cows and sheep from spermatid-like haploid embryonic stem cells
summary: Nature Biotechnology published a correction to a previous article on generating modified cows and sheep from spermatid-like haploid embryonic stem cells. The correction, published online June 25, 2026, fixes a figure preparation error in the original Fig. 3d where the bottom-right immunohistochemistry image incorrectly showed a sheep-sheep chimera instead of a cow-cow chimera as originally intended. The corrected figure now appears consistently in both HTML and PDF versions of the article. The correction also marks the passing of late author Li Zhang. Original authors include Lei Yang, Shaorong Gao, Guangpeng Li and collaborators from Inner Mongolia University, Tongji University, and Northeast Agricultural University.
analysis_notes: This is an author correction (erratum), not a substantive research article. It corrects a figure labeling error in the original paper (DOI 10.1038/s41587-025-02832-4, published Oct 7, 2025). No new data or findings are reported. Academic rather than commercial biotech relevance.

Correction to: _Nature Biotechnology_ [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-025-02832-4](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-025-02832-4), published online 7 October 2025.

In the version of this article initially published, due to a figure preparation mistake, the bottom-right immunohistochemistry images in Fig. 3d presented a sheep–sheep chimera, whereas it should have shown a cow–cow chimera, as now appears in the HTML and PDF versions of the article. For comparison, the original Fig. [3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03216-y#MOESM1) is available as Supplementary Information accompanying this amendment.

title: Unlocking the chemical potential of filamentous fungi using prime editing
summary: Sun et al. report fPE7max, a prime editor optimized for filamentous fungi that enables precise genome engineering ranging from single-base substitutions to kilobase-scale insertions and multi-kilobase deletions across diverse fungal species. The team demonstrated that editing translation-regulatory elements—specifically upstream open reading frames (uORFs) in a pleiotropic metabolic regulator gene—can unlock previously inaccessible biosynthetic pathways. This approach uncovered new fungal metabolites that arise only after regulatory element disruption, expanding the tractable chemical space of fungal natural products. For biotech operators, the work means prime editing can replace traditional knockout or promoter-swap strategies to activate cryptic gene clusters in industrial filamentous fungi such as Aspergillus and Trichoderma, which are major hosts for enzyme and antibiotic production. The method is broadly applicable across fungal species and does not rely on double-strand breaks, reducing the indels that have historically limited editing efficiency in heterokaryotic molds.
analysis_notes: Content is limited to a Nature Biotechnology news/summary preview behind a paywall. Title derived from the Cite this article section; the actual research paper is by Sun et al. (doi: 10.1038/s41587-026-03202-4). The summary was constructed from the visible abstract paragraph, figure caption, and reference annotations, supplemented with contextual knowledge about prime editing in fungi.

We developed the prime editor fPE7max, which is optimized for filamentous fungi and enables precise genome engineering — from single-base substitutions to kilobase-scale insertions and multi-kilobase deletions, across diverse species. Editing of translation-regulatory elements for a pleiotropic metabolic regulator unlocked previously inaccessible biosynthetic pathways and uncovered new fungal metabolites.

title: Publisher Correction: Lung and liver editing by lipid nanoparticle delivery of a stable CRISPR–Cas9 ribonucleoprotein
summary: This is a Publisher Correction to an original article published 16 October 2024, not a primary research piece. The correction amends a single Methods sentence in the ‘Directed evolution of GeoCas9’ section: replacing ‘sulfur-oxidizing bacteria’ with ‘SOB medium (Super Optimal Broth)’ for the bacterial recovery step after electroporation. The original work reports CRISPR–Cas9 ribonucleoprotein delivery via lipid nanoparticles for lung and liver genome editing, led by Kai Chen, Hesong Han, Niren Murthy, and Jennifer A. Doudna at UC Berkeley and the Innovative Genomics Institute. As a procedural correction only, it carries no new data or scientific findings worth acting on.
analysis_notes: Publisher correction only (textual Methods fix); no new results, deals, approvals, or clinical outcomes. Original article DOI: 10.1038/s41587-024-02437-3.

Correction to: _Nature Biotechnology_ [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-024-02437-3](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-024-02437-3), published online 16 October 2024.

In the version of the article initially published, in the “Directed evolution of GeoCas9” section of the Methods, the text “After recovery of the electroporated bacteria in 750 µl of sulfur-oxidizing bacteria for 1.5 h at 30 °C” should have read “After recovery of the electroporated bacteria in 750 µl of SOB medium (Super Optimal Broth) for 1.5 h at 30 °C.” This correction has been made to the HTML and PDF versions of the article.

title: Quantum computing in transition
summary: A Nature Biotechnology perspective argues that near-term quantum advantage in biotech will come from hybrid quantum-classical systems, not fully fault-tolerant quantum machines. Concrete milestones include encoding the full hepatitis D virus genome on IBM quantum hardware and Q4Bio challenge results demonstrating protein complex simulations exceeding 12,000 atoms — the largest known simulations of biologically meaningful molecules to date. IBM predicts 2026 as the inflection point for practical quantum advantage via hybrid approaches, while Google's Willow chip has demonstrated sub-threshold error rates enabling exponential logical error suppression. The article warns that sensitive genomic data faces 'harvest now, decrypt later' quantum cryptanalysis threats, and post-quantum cryptographic standards from NIST (2024) urgently need adoption in biological databases. Finally, the piece flags a 'quantum divide': without deliberate global initiatives like UNESCO's proposal, early quantum advantage will accrue only to well-funded U.S., European, and Chinese institutions, neglecting tropical disease research and antimicrobial challenges in low-income settings.

[Skip to main content](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x#content)

Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain
the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in
Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles
and JavaScript.

Quantum computing in transition


[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x.pdf)

[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x.pdf)

Hybrid quantum–classical computers will achieve quantum advantage in biotechnology, bridging the time until a full quantum computer becomes available.

The year 2025 was declared the International Year of Quantum Science and Technology by the United Nations General Assembly, celebrating 100 years of quantum science. Yet, amid this excitement surrounding quantum computers and their promise to revolutionize medicine, chemistry and biotechnology across the globe, an important reality is often overlooked: quantum advantage — the state in which a quantum computer solves a practical, real-world problem faster or more efficiently than the most powerful classical supercomputer — is unlikely to emerge from a fully quantum machine at first. It will instead come from hybrid quantum–classical computing platforms that combine the strengths of quantum processors with the scale and reliability of classical computing.

In fact, there have already been exciting developments in the application of quantum computing to biotechnology achieved by hybrid quantum–classical systems. Researchers have successfully encoded and analyzed the entire genome of the hepatitis D virus on IBM quantum hardware[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x#ref-CR1 "Offord, C. Science                  https://doi.org/10.1126/science.z9ookm7                                 (2026)."). Results from the international Quantum for Bio ( [Q4Bio](https://wellcomeleap.org/q4bio/)) challenge showed that hybrid quantum–classical methods can contribute to molecular simulation and drug-discovery problems, including simulation of protein complexes over 12,000 atoms — the largest known simulations of biologically meaningful molecules[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x#ref-CR2 "Merz, K. M. et al. Preprint at arXiv                  https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2605.01138                                 (2026)."). In January this year, IBM predicted that 2026 will be the inflection point where we will see a true quantum advantage using hybrid systems.

Current quantum computers are powerful but incomplete tools, and biological systems are complex. Quantum computers are not yet able to address biological challenges independently and at scale. Qubits, the fundamental unit of information in a quantum computer, are error-prone — vibrations or slight variations in temperature or electromagnetic field can cause loss of coherence. Current quantum computers are noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices: they have dozens or a few hundred qubits but are not error-corrected and introduce errors into their operations.

Hybrid systems use quantum error mitigation, reducing the impact of noise on quantum computations without requiring full error correction. Classical computers and quantum computers are tightly coupled in iterative loops where only small parts of the computation are performed on the quantum hardware and the classical computer is used to handle errors, perform optimization and process data. These systems act as a bridge to achieving quantum advantage, but they are not likely to scale well to the number of qubits required for use cases that are projected to eventually benefit most from quantum computing, such as molecular simulation and drug design, or protein or RNA folding.

Error correction techniques, by contrast, achieve physical correction of quantum states while maintaining the phase relationship between quantum states. Combining multiple physical qubits into a logical qubit detects and corrects errors without directly measuring the qubits’ states. However, a critical challenge here is that the correction itself adds more qubits and more error-prone operations to the system; therefore, the base error rate needs to be below a certain threshold to make error correction advantageous. Google’s Willow chip achieves this sub-threshold error rate[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x#ref-CR3 "Google Quantum AI and Collaborators. Nature                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-024-08449-y                                 (2025)."). As more qubits are added with this type of correction, the logical error rate is suppressed exponentially — hinting at possible routes to building a large-scale fault-tolerant quantum computer, with estimates for realization as soon as 2030.

While researchers pursue the long-term goal of fault-tolerant quantum computers, the pharmaceutical industry has adopted a more pragmatic perspective, extracting useful insights from the quantum technologies available now and investing heavily in collaborations with quantum hardware providers so as to get ready to make use of a full-scale quantum computer once it becomes available. As these companies have access to sensitive intellectual property and clinical data, they must also consider the security risk. Sensitive encrypted data can be intercepted and stored using a ‘harvest now, decrypt later’ strategy, and once a sufficiently powerful quantum computer becomes available it will be able to be deciphered. Resource estimates for breaking RSA-2048, a commonly used encryption scheme, have dropped from around 20 million physical qubits to potentially fewer than 100,000 with new algorithms and architectures[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x#ref-CR4 "Cain et al. Preprint at arXiv                  https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2603.28627                                 (2026)."), [5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x#ref-CR5 "Babbush et al. Preprint at arXiv                  https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2603.28846                                 (2026)"). Genomic and health data do not expire and are uniquely exposed to this threat: while the data themselves are often available, they are anonymized and the information needed to deanonymize them is encrypted. Data collected today and decrypted in 2035 will be as sensitive as on the day they were created — maybe more so, given that genomic analysis techniques will be more advanced. Post-quantum cryptographic standards have been available[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x#ref-CR6 "National Institute of Standards and Technology. Federal Register                  https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2024/08/14/2024-17956/announcing-issuance-of-federal-information-processing-standards-fips-fips-203-module-lattice-based                                 (2024).") since 2024, introducing encryption methods that are designed to be secure against attacks by future quantum computers. Biological databases need to develop timelines for adoption to protect sensitive data.

Biotechnology already occupies a central role in economic competitiveness, healthcare innovation and national security. As these sectors converge in the realm of quantum computing, policymakers will face questions concerning infrastructure, workforce development, intellectual property and equitable access. Early engagement will be essential to ensure that scientific opportunity translates into societal benefit.

Technologies that are strategically sensitive and capital-intensive tend to concentrate in a limited number of hands, raising the possibility that quantum advantage may not be evenly distributed across the global scientific and industrial landscape. The institutions with early access — a handful of large pharmaceutical companies, national labs and well-funded universities in the United States, Europe and China — will gain first-mover advantages in identification of structurally difficult drug target identification, for example. This means that the first wave of quantum-enabled drug discoveries will almost certainly address diseases in areas where the institutions with quantum access have financial incentives to direct their computing. Neglected tropical diseases and antimicrobial resistance in low-income settings will not be early beneficiaries of quantum-accelerated biology. This is not an inevitable outcome, but it is the default trajectory unless explicitly countered by global initiatives such as the [one proposed by UNESCO](https://www.unesco.org/en/global-quantum-initiative), which aims to address this ‘quantum divide’ by providing an open platform for dialog, cooperation and capacity building.

Quantum computing is unlikely to transform biotechnology in a single leap. Instead, its impact is emerging through hybrid systems that combine quantum and classical strengths, delivering incremental advantage while full fault tolerance remains out of reach. The challenge is not only to build quantum advantage, but to ensure it arrives in a form that can be securely, responsibly and equitably used.

title: Author Correction: Improved RNA base editing with guide RNAs mimicking highly edited endogenous ADAR substrates
summary: This is an author correction (corrigendum) to a prior Nature Biotechnology article on improved RNA base editing using guide RNAs inspired by highly edited endogenous ADAR substrates. The correction fixes a data duplication error in Figure 5a of the original paper: the A549 cell line data had been incorrectly duplicated from U2OS data due to a figure assembly error. The corrected A549 data has now been substituted in both HTML and PDF versions. The underlying research, from Sun Yat-Sen University and RecoRNA Biotechnology, explores RNA editing technology built around engineered guide RNAs that leverage ADAR enzyme biology. This correction does not add substantive new findings; it amends a figure panel only.
analysis_notes: Not a substantive research article. This is purely a corrigendum correcting duplicated data in one figure panel of the parent paper (doi: 10.1038/s41587-025-02628-6). No new scientific results, clinical trials, commercial deals, or regulatory developments are reported here. The original paper likely carries more intelligence value.

title: Author Correction: Sustained nitric oxide production by engineered E. coli remodels the tumor microenvironment and potentiates immunotherapy
summary: Nature Biotechnology published an author correction (06 July 2026) for a study on engineered E. coli producing sustained nitric oxide to remodel the tumor microenvironment and enhance immunotherapy in cancer. The original article appeared on 18 March 2026 (DOI: 10.1038/s41587-026-03054-y) by researchers at Peking University Third Hospital and the Agency for Science, Technology and Research. The correction addresses data integrity issues: an errant anti-EGFR dataset was removed from Fig. 4n source data; PBS group measurement errors in Fig. 6d affecting related plots were fixed; data entry errors in Figs. 4o and 5b–d were resolved; and inconsistent decimal-place formatting in Figs. 1a and 1b was corrected. Source Data files and the Fig. 6d plot have been revised in both HTML and PDF versions of the article.

Author Correction: Sustained nitric oxide production by engineered _E. coli_ remodels the tumor microenvironment and potentiates immunotherapy


[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03247-5.pdf)

[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03247-5.pdf)

The [Original Article](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-026-03054-y) was published on 18 March 2026

Correction to: _Nature Biotechnology_ [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-026-03054-y](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-026-03054-y), published online 18 March 2026.

In the version of this article initially published, there was an error in the Fig. 4n Source Data, where an additional “anti-EGFR” dataset was accidentally included; an error in the Fig. 6d Source Data, with errors in the PBS group measurements, also affecting the plots in the article; errors in the Figs. 4o, 5b–d, h Source Data, due to data entry errors; and errors in the Fig. 1a, b Source Data, where decimal-place formatting was inconsistent. The Source Data and Fig. 6d have been revised in the HTML and PDF versions of the article.
